name: She walks down the hallway
content: She walks down one direction of the hallway and I up the other. The distance between our steps is five seconds, maybe, but I have a short temperament for anxiety. It was another two seconds ago when the vague-haze-mundanity first made its way into my cognition. She was a shadow, or the general shape of a person. From all this way, I couldn’t tell quite how tall she was in actuality, only that in relation to the walls on both sides of her and the ceiling above. It doesn’t help that the floor is reflective; there are now two of me and two of her. Breadth becomes decipherable.

Two seconds ago, the probability of a random person being her given that they were walking down this hallway right now was lower than it is right now. There are many people at this university, but fewer women, and then with hair that length and irises that colour. Cortisol rises. If in my right hand I held, right at this moment, a glassful of wine, sips would splatter onto the mirror below. My stature does not change, and in fact I have trained even my eyelids to retain their default sag in this situation. The distance my breath travels down my chest shortens. Mouth-breathing does not help. It is not a heaviness—not exactly, no. If they had time my teeth should clatter. Glugs of water, by the way, tend also to bring this sort of waning heaviness that feels like it’s going to make me feel like throwing up. With no time nor water I stride still.

Now I want to avoid imagining things. This is because there are certainty signals, which, if detected, would diagnose her immediately, and we want not to hallucinate these. One incorrect tinge of a Jo Malone, for instance, would spell dire consequences not by causality but by foretelling. The way she pairs fibres in her outfit is a certainty signal. Shitty shoes would give it away, too. We come closer. Complexion checks out. In my head I can make some sense of these investigations because I convince myself that if I, as a sampled populace unit, have these thoughts then so much she. If she plays this game in her head then the both of us must be very good at it since neither can tell. If she can tell then she must be smarter than I am.

A key strategy here is to continue to face forward. Since the both of us walk on the American side of the road, we should swipe by. It is important to fixate on the source of the light that she is walking away from during this manoeuvre. Again, she may also have better things to do than obsess over the probability of a given sight being that which is mine. Perhaps she doesn’t think about me. I don’t think about her, either, but am—I think—averse to the horrors that may arise if we come face-to-face. I don’t know what these horrors are and I do not want to begin to manufacture them in my head. Again, I want to avoid imagining things.
